stem: Where should you mainly focus your eyes while driving on an open road?

choices.text: On the bumper of the car ahead
On the road markings just in front of your car
As far ahead as you can clearly see
On your speedometer

explanation.short: Look as far ahead as you can; this gives you the most time to plan.
explanation.examTip: Short-sighted looking causes late reactions and is a frequent test fault.

stem: How often should you glance at your interior mirror in normal driving?

choices.text: Only when braking
Roughly every 5 to 8 seconds and before every change of speed or direction
Only when you suspect someone is following you
Only on motorways

explanation.short: Glance at the mirror roughly every 5–8 seconds and before each speed or direction change.
explanation.examTip: Examiners specifically watch your mirror-use rhythm.

stem: You drive past a row of parked cars on a residential street. The greatest hidden hazard is:

choices.text: A police speed trap behind the cars
An oil patch on the road surface
A pedestrian or child stepping out between cars
A puncture from a piece of glass

explanation.short: A pedestrian or child emerging between parked cars is the classic hidden hazard.
explanation.examTip: Reduce speed and increase distance from the kerb when passing parked rows.

stem: How much sideways space should you give a cyclist when overtaking inside a built-up area?

choices.text: At least 1 metre
About 30 cm — the same as parking
Whatever the lane width allows
No gap is required if you sound the horn

explanation.short: Give cyclists at least 1 metre of side space — 1.5 m where speed is higher.
explanation.examTip: Cyclists may wobble or swerve around a parked car; expect it.

stem: You approach a school zone at the end of the school day. You should:

choices.text: Maintain speed because children are supervised
Reduce speed early and expect sudden movements off the kerb
Use the horn to warn children to keep back
Stop fully at every junction

explanation.short: Reduce speed early near schools; children may step out unexpectedly.
explanation.examTip: Anticipate before you see the hazard, not after.

stem: On a rural road with no pavement, on which side do pedestrians walk?

choices.text: On the right, with the traffic
On the left, facing oncoming traffic
Down the centre of the road
They are not allowed to walk on such roads

explanation.short: Pedestrians walk on the left, facing oncoming traffic, so they can see vehicles.
explanation.examTip: Dip headlights and keep well to your side of the road at dusk.

stem: When is a road typically most slippery after a dry spell?

choices.text: After several hours of heavy rain has washed it clean
During the first minutes of rain after a long dry period
Only when temperatures fall below freezing
Only on motorways

explanation.short: First rain after a dry spell lifts oil and dust to the surface, making the road most slippery.
explanation.examTip: Increase following distance and brake earlier as soon as drops start.

stem: When may you switch on your rear fog light?

choices.text: Whenever it is raining
Only when visibility is less than 50 metres
At any time on a motorway
Only between dusk and dawn

explanation.short: Rear fog lights are allowed only when visibility falls below 50 m.
explanation.examTip: Switch them off as soon as visibility improves — they dazzle the driver behind you.

stem: What does 'do not outdrive your headlights' mean?

choices.text: Always use full beam on unlit roads
Your stopping distance must fit inside the area you can see lit
Only drive at night with two headlights working
Avoid braking once you have switched on the headlights

explanation.short: Your stopping distance must fit inside the beam you can see; otherwise slow down.
explanation.examTip: On unlit rural roads this often means well below the legal limit.

stem: Roughly how long does an alert, healthy driver need to react to an unexpected hazard?

choices.text: About 0.1 second
About 1 second
About 3 seconds
Reaction is instant once you see the hazard

explanation.short: Alert drivers need roughly 1 second to react before braking even starts.
explanation.examTip: Tired, distracted or impaired drivers need noticeably longer.

stem: In good conditions, what is the minimum recommended time-gap to the vehicle in front?

choices.text: 0.5 second
1 second
2 seconds
5 seconds

explanation.short: Keep at least a 2-second gap in good conditions.
explanation.examTip: Double the gap in rain; increase further in snow or fog.

stem: You see a ball roll into the road from between parked cars. The safest response is to:

choices.text: Continue at speed; balls do not damage cars
Brake sharply only if you see the child
Ease off, cover the brake, and be ready to stop for a child
Swerve into the oncoming lane

explanation.short: Treat a ball as a warning that a child may follow — ease off and cover the brake.
explanation.examTip: Anticipating like this means you almost never have to brake hard.

stem: How often should you glance in your mirrors while driving on an open road?

choices.text: Only before braking
Roughly every 5–8 seconds and before every manoeuvre
Only when overtaking
Once per minute is enough

explanation.short: Regular mirror checks every few seconds keep your picture of traffic behind you fresh.
explanation.examTip: Mirrors–signal–manoeuvre is the standard sequence examiners look for.

stem: You pass a primary school just before 8:30 in the morning. Several children are on the pavement. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain speed and use the horn
Reduce speed and watch for children stepping into the road
Move to the centre of the road
Stop and wave them across

explanation.short: Children act unpredictably near schools; lower your speed and cover the brake.
explanation.examTip: The same care applies after school and around sport clubs and playgrounds.

stem: Visibility drops to about 50 metres in dense fog. Which lights should you use?

choices.text: High beam to see further
Dipped headlights and front fog lights; rear fog light is allowed
Hazard warning lights only
Side lights only

explanation.short: Use dipped beam (high beam reflects in fog), front fog lights as needed, and the rear fog light only when visibility is under 50 m.
explanation.examTip: Switch the rear fog off the moment visibility improves — it dazzles drivers behind you.

stem: You drive along a row of parked cars in a residential street. Which is the most useful clue that a car may be about to pull out?

choices.text: Brake lights or exhaust smoke
A clean windscreen
Empty roof racks
Number plate colour

explanation.short: Brake lights, exhaust fumes, front wheels turned outward and a driver in the seat all signal a possible pull-out.
explanation.examTip: Open doors and pedestrians arriving with keys are other early warnings.

stem: Inside town at 50 km/h you approach a parked ice-cream van on your right, with children visible nearby. A cyclist is following close behind you. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Keep your speed — the children are on the pavement, not the road
Slow well before the van and check the mirror so the cyclist sees you brake early
Sound your horn to warn the children
Swerve into the oncoming lane to give the van extra clearance

explanation.short: Children near a parked van are a high-risk hazard; slow early and warn the follower with brake lights, not the horn.
explanation.examTip: Anticipate the hidden pedestrian — do not wait until a child steps out.

stem: On a winter morning the dashboard shows +2 °C. You approach a bridge in a shaded valley. The road looks dry. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain speed — +2 °C is above freezing, so no risk
Brake hard just before the bridge to test grip
Slow before the bridge and avoid sudden braking or steering on it
Use main beam to see the bridge surface

explanation.short: Bridges and shaded patches freeze first; +2 °C is well within the surface-ice risk window.
explanation.examTip: Treat shadowed tarmac and bridges in winter as potentially icy, even when the rest of the road looks dry.

stem: You are on a wet motorway following a lorry at 80 km/h with a 4-second gap. A car cuts into the gap between you and the lorry. What is the best response?

choices.text: Flash your headlights and accelerate to close the gap behind
Brake hard immediately to teach the driver a lesson
Ease off gently to reopen at least 4 seconds to the new car ahead
Overtake on the right to get past the car

explanation.short: Restore the gap by dropping back, not by braking hard or accelerating.
explanation.examTip: Tailgaters and cut-ins are managed by widening the gap in front of you — never by closing it.

stem: On a busy shopping street at 30 km/h you see: a traffic light in the distance turning amber, a bus pulling out of a stop ahead, a pedestrian at the kerb on your right, and a cyclist closing in your mirror. Which is the correct way to handle the picture?

choices.text: Focus only on the traffic light — it is the legal control
Treat all four as active hazards and adjust speed early
Ignore the cyclist behind — what is ahead matters more
Speed up to clear the bus before it pulls out

explanation.short: Active scanning means tracking far, mid, near and mirror at the same time; all four cues matter.
explanation.examTip: The CBR hazard-perception part rewards drivers who name multiple early cues, not just the most obvious one.
